subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 10! In this unit you will learn how to turn a pile of information into a clear picture that anyone can read at a glance. That picture is called a graph. Every day the world is full of information, or data: how many goats came home, how many mangoes were sold at the Hargeisa market, how many children walked to Nuur Academy this morning. On its own, a long list of numbers is hard to understand. A graph takes that list and shows it in a way your eyes can read in a single moment.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 3 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Read and interpret data shown in a pictogram, using the key to work out real amounts.
Draw a pictogram from given data, choosing a sensible symbol and key.
Read and interpret a bar chart (block graph), including its title, axes and scale.
Draw a bar chart with equal bars, equal gaps and a scale that starts at zero.
Choose a sensible scale (counting in 1s, 2s, 5s or 10s) for a set of data.
Sort items and numbers into a Venn diagram, including the overlapping middle.
Sort items and numbers into a Carroll diagram using yes/no criteria.
Answer real questions from data: most, least, total, and 'how many more'.
Choose the most suitable graph for a given set of data and say why.

concepts.title: What Is Data and Why We Show It
concepts.explanation: Before we draw a single graph, let us be sure what data means. Data is simply information you have collected. If Amina asks every child in her class which fruit they like best and writes down each answer, she has collected data. If Hassan counts how many goats, camels and cattle his family owns and writes the three numbers down, he has collected data too. Data usually starts life as a messy list or a set of tally marks. The trouble with a raw list is that it is hard to read. Suppose Amina's list says: mango,…
concepts.example: Here is Amina's list tidied into a frequency table: Mango appeared 5 times, Banana 3 times, and Orange 2 times. Now, at a glance, you can see mango is the most popular. A frequency table is the sensible first step…

concepts.title: Pictograms and the Key
concepts.explanation: A pictogram is a graph that uses pictures or symbols to show data. Each picture stands for a certain number of items. Pictograms are friendly and fun to look at, which is why you often see them in books for younger children and in colourful posters. Look at this pictogram showing how many books some children read in one week. Each 📚 symbol stands for 2 books. This little note, 'one symbol = 2 books', is called the key, and it is the most important part of any pictogram.
concepts.example: Amina – 📚📚📚 (3 symbols); Farah – 📚📚📚📚📚 (5 symbols); Hassan – 📚📚 (2 symbols); Yusuf – 📚📚📚📚 (4 symbols). Key: one 📚 = 2 books.

concepts.title: Bar Charts and Block Graphs
concepts.explanation: A bar chart uses bars to compare amounts. The taller (or longer) the bar, the bigger the amount. When the bars are made of single squares stacked one on top of another, we sometimes call it a block graph, because each block is worth one. A bar chart is one of the most useful graphs of all, because it makes comparing amounts very easy: you simply look for the tallest and shortest bars. Every bar chart has two axes (say 'ax-eez'). The across axis (the horizontal line along the bottom) shows the categories, such as…
concepts.example: Here is a bar chart of favourite fruits in Farah's class. Reading up the side scale, the Mango bar reaches 5, the Banana bar reaches 3, and the Orange bar reaches 2. So 5 children chose mango, 3 chose banana and 2 chose…

concepts.title: Choosing a Sensible Scale
concepts.explanation: The scale is how much each step on the up axis is worth. Choosing a good scale is one of the most important skills in this whole unit, so let us take it slowly. If your data has small numbers, such as 2, 3 and 5, then counting up in 1s (0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5) is perfect, because every value fits neatly. But if your data has larger numbers, such as 40, 90 and 120, counting in 1s would need a chart taller than a wall. Instead you would count in 10s or even 20s. Here is a friendly rule. Look at the biggest number in your…
concepts.example: Why must the scale start at zero? Because if it starts higher, the bars can trick your eyes. Imagine a scale that started at 90 for the museum visitors. A day with 100 visitors and a day with 120 visitors would look…

concepts.title: Venn Diagrams
concepts.explanation: A Venn diagram uses circles to sort items into groups. What makes it special is the overlap: where two circles cross, we place the items that belong to both groups at once. Venn diagrams are wonderful for showing how groups are connected. Picture two circles. The left circle is labelled 'Even numbers' and the right circle is labelled 'Multiples of 3'. We sort the numbers 2, 4, 8, 9, 15, 21, 6, 12 and 18. The even numbers that are not multiples of 3, such as 2, 4 and 8, go in the left-only part. The multiples of 3…
concepts.example: The overlap is the heart of the Venn diagram. To decide where a number goes, ask two questions: Does it fit the left circle? Does it fit the right circle? If the answer is yes to both, it goes in the middle. If yes to…

concepts.title: Carroll Diagrams
concepts.explanation: A Carroll diagram is another way to sort, but instead of circles it uses a grid of boxes. It sorts items by asking yes or no questions. It is named after Lewis Carroll, who wrote *Alice in Wonderland* and loved puzzles and mathematics. A simple Carroll diagram has two columns and two rows. Suppose we sort the numbers 1 to 12 by two rules: 'Even?' across the top and 'More than 6?' down the side. The top row is 'More than 6' and the bottom row is 'Not more than 6'. The left column is 'Even' and the right column is…
concepts.example: The clever thing about a Carroll diagram is that every item has a home. Each box answers both questions at once: yes-yes, yes-no, no-yes, or no-no. Nothing can fall between the boxes, and nothing can be left out,…

methods.title: How to read a pictogram
methods.example: A pictogram shows cupcakes sold. Key: one 🧁 = 5 cupcakes. Monday shows 4 symbols, Tuesday shows 2 symbols. How many cupcakes on each day, and how many altogether?
steps: Read the key to see what one symbol is worth.
Count the whole symbols in a row and multiply by the key value.
Add on any part symbols (a half is worth half the key).
Write the real amount.

methods.title: How to draw a bar chart
methods.example: Key: one 🐪 = 10 camels. Liban's herd is shown as 3 whole camels and 1 half camel. How many camels?
steps: Tidy the data into a frequency table.
Choose a sensible scale for the biggest number, starting at zero.
Draw and label both axes and give the chart a title.
Draw equal-width bars with equal gaps, using a ruler.

methods.title: How to choose a scale
methods.example: A bar chart of favourite colours has a scale counting in 2s. The Blue bar reaches 8 and the Green bar reaches halfway between 4 and 6. How many chose each?
steps: Find the biggest number in the data.
Up to 10 count in 1s; up to 20 count in 2s; up to 50 count in 5s; 100 or more count in 10s.
Make sure every step is the same size and starts at zero.

methods.title: Choose a scale
methods.example: Omar's mango sales are 20, 35, 15 and 40. Which scale is sensible: counting in 1s, in 5s, or in unequal steps of 0, 10, 30, 40?
steps: Solution: Counting in 5s (0, 5, 10, ..., 40) is sensible: the steps are equal and it reaches 40 without too many lines.
Counting in 1s would need 40 lines, far too many.
The unequal steps 0, 10, 30, 40 are not allowed, because the gaps are different sizes and would make the chart dishonest.

methods.title: Sort into a Venn diagram
methods.example: Sort 4, 7, 12, 15 into a Venn diagram: left circle 'Even', right circle 'More than 10'.
steps: Solution: 4 is even but not more than 10, so left-only.
7 is neither even nor more than 10, so outside both.
12 is even and more than 10, so it goes in the overlap.
15 is not even but is more than 10, so right-only.

methods.title: Sort into a Carroll diagram
methods.example: Sort 6 and 9 into a Carroll diagram with columns 'Even / Not even' and rows 'More than 5 / Not more than 5'.
steps: Solution: For 6: it is even (left column) and more than 5 (top row), so top-left box.
For 9: it is not even (right column) and more than 5 (top row), so top-right box.
Doing the two questions in order gives each number its home.
Worked Example 7: How many more?

workedExamples.title: Read a pictogram
workedExamples.prompt: A pictogram shows cupcakes sold. Key: one 🧁 = 5 cupcakes. Monday shows 4 symbols, Tuesday shows 2 symbols. How many cupcakes on each day, and how many altogether?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Multiply symbols by the key value of 5. Monday: 4 × 5 = 20 cupcakes. Tuesday: 2 × 5 = 10 cupcakes. Altogether: 20 + 10 = 30 cupcakes. The key of 5 is what turns the pictures into real numbers.

workedExamples.title: Use half a symbol
workedExamples.prompt: Key: one 🐪 = 10 camels. Liban's herd is shown as 3 whole camels and 1 half camel. How many camels?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 3 whole symbols = 3 × 10 = 30. A half symbol is worth half of 10, which is 5. So 30 + 5 = 35 camels. Part symbols let a pictogram show numbers between the key steps.

workedExamples.title: Read a bar chart
workedExamples.prompt: A bar chart of favourite colours has a scale counting in 2s. The Blue bar reaches 8 and the Green bar reaches halfway between 4 and 6. How many chose each?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Blue reaches the line marked 8, so 8 children chose blue. Green sits halfway between 4 and 6, which is 5 children. Remember that a bar ending between two marks is read as the value in between.

workedExamples.title: Choose a scale
workedExamples.prompt: Omar's mango sales are 20, 35, 15 and 40. Which scale is sensible: counting in 1s, in 5s, or in unequal steps of 0, 10, 30, 40?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Counting in 5s (0, 5, 10, ..., 40) is sensible: the steps are equal and it reaches 40 without too many lines. Counting in 1s would need 40 lines, far too many. The unequal steps 0, 10, 30, 40 are not allowed, because the gaps are different sizes and would make the chart dishonest.

workedExamples.title: Sort into a Venn diagram
workedExamples.prompt: Sort 4, 7, 12, 15 into a Venn diagram: left circle 'Even', right circle 'More than 10'.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 4 is even but not more than 10, so left-only. 7 is neither even nor more than 10, so outside both. 12 is even and more than 10, so it goes in the overlap. 15 is not even but is more than 10, so right-only. Always ask both questions for each number.

workedExamples.title: Sort into a Carroll diagram
workedExamples.prompt: Sort 6 and 9 into a Carroll diagram with columns 'Even / Not even' and rows 'More than 5 / Not more than 5'.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: For 6: it is even (left column) and more than 5 (top row), so top-left box. For 9: it is not even (right column) and more than 5 (top row), so top-right box. Doing the two questions in order gives each number its home. Worked Example 7: How many more? A bar chart of museum visitors shows Friday with 200 and Tuesday with 90. How many more visitors came on Friday than on Tuesday? Solution: 'How many more' means subtract the smaller from the larger. 200 − 90 = 110 more visitors on Friday. Subtraction is the…

workedExamples.title: How many more?
workedExamples.prompt: Sort 6 and 9 into a Carroll diagram with columns 'Even / Not even' and rows 'More than 5 / Not more than 5'.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: For 6: it is even (left column) and more than 5 (top row), so top-left box. For 9: it is not even (right column) and more than 5 (top row), so top-right box. Doing the two questions in order gives each number its home. Worked Example 7: How many more? A bar chart of museum visitors shows Friday with 200 and Tuesday with 90. How many more visitors came on Friday than on Tuesday? Solution: 'How many more' means subtract the smaller from the larger. 200 − 90 = 110 more visitors on Friday. Subtraction is the…

workedExamples.title: Find the total
workedExamples.prompt: A bar chart shows visitors: Monday 120, Wednesday 150, Friday 200. What was the total for these three days?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add the bar values. 120 + 150 + 200 = 470 visitors in total. To find a total from any graph, read every bar and add them all together.

workedExamples.title: Choose a sensible scale for each biggest number
workedExamples.prompt: Choose a sensible scale for each biggest number: a) 9 b) 18 c) 45 d) 100. Explain each.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: Sort these numbers into a Venn diagram of 'Even' and 'Multiple of 3': 2, 3, 6, 9, 8, 12.
workedExamples.solution: a) 1s, b) 2s, c) 5s, d) 10s – each keeps equal steps without too many lines.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: A bar chart of visitors shows Monday 120, Friday 200, Saturday 250. How many more came on Saturday than on Monday?

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: A pictogram key is one 🍎 = 4 apples. A row is worth 26 apples. How many whole and part symbols are drawn?

activities.title: Household Survey Bar Chart
activities.materials: paper, a ruler, a pencil, and some people to ask.
steps: Choose a question, such as 'What is your favourite fruit?' with four choices.
Ask at least eight people and tally their answers into a frequency table.
Choose a sensible scale for the biggest number, starting at zero.
Draw a bar chart with a title, labelled axes, equal bars and equal gaps.
Write two things your chart shows, such as the most and least popular choice.

activities.title: Make a Pictogram with a Key
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a simple symbol you can draw many times.
steps: Count something at home, such as spoons, cups and plates.
Choose a key, for example one symbol = 2 items, so you do not draw too many.
Draw neat rows of symbols, using a half symbol where you need an odd number.
Write a clear title and the key underneath.
Ask a family member to read your pictogram and check they get the right amounts.

activities.title: The Scale-Choosing Game
activities.materials: paper number cards, or numbers written on scraps of paper.
steps: Shuffle the cards and turn over four numbers.
Find the biggest number among them.
Decide the best scale: 1s, 2s, 5s or 10s, so the biggest number fits neatly.
Say your reason out loud, then swap and check a partner's choice.
This game trains you to pick a sensible scale quickly, a skill every graph needs.

activities.title: Venn Diagram Sorting with Stones
activities.materials: a handful of stones, beans or bottle caps, and two loops of string.
steps: Lay the two string loops on the ground so they overlap in the middle.
Label one loop 'round' and the other 'grey' (or any two properties).
Sort each object: if it fits both, place it in the overlap; if neither, place it outside.
Count how many are in each part and talk about what the overlap means.

activities.title: Carroll Diagram Number Hunt (Family Game)
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and the numbers 1 to 12 written on cards.
steps: Draw a two-by-two grid with columns 'Even / Not even' and rows 'More than 6 / Not more than 6'.
Take turns drawing a number card and asking the two yes/no questions aloud.
Place the card in the correct box.
When all cards are placed, check that every box has the right numbers.
Activity 6: Investigation – Which Graph Tells the Story Best?

activities.title: Investigation – Which Graph Tells the Story Best?
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and the numbers 1 to 12 written on cards.
steps: Draw a two-by-two grid with columns 'Even / Not even' and rows 'More than 6 / Not more than 6'.
Take turns drawing a number card and asking the two yes/no questions aloud.
Place the card in the correct box.
When all cards are placed, check that every box has the right numbers.
Activity 6: Investigation – Which Graph Tells the Story Best?

rules.title: Pictogram Rule
rules.text: Number of items = number of symbols multiplied by the key value. A half symbol is worth half the key. Read the key before the pictures.

rules.title: Bar Chart Rule
rules.text: Across axis shows the categories. Up axis shows the amount. Start the number axis at zero and use equal steps. Read a value by sliding from the top of the bar across to the up axis.

rules.title: Sorting Rule
rules.text: Venn diagram: items that fit both groups go in the overlapping middle. Carroll diagram: sort by two yes/no questions; every item has one box.

terms: A graph using pictures or symbols
What each symbol in a pictogram is worth
A graph using bars to compare amounts
A labelled line on the side or bottom
How much each step on an axis is worth
Circles that sort groups and overlaps
A grid that sorts by yes/no rules
commonMistakes: Counting symbols, not using the key
Multiply symbols by the key value
Always start the number axis at zero
Overlap item in one circle only
Place both-group items in the middle

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A pictogram key is one 🍎 = 4 apples. A row is worth 26 apples. How many whole and part symbols are drawn?

reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A Venn diagram uses circles to sort items into groups. What makes it special is the overlap: where two circles cross, we place the items that belong to both groups at once. Venn diagrams are wonderful for showing how groups are connected. The even numbers that are not multiples of 3, such as 2, 4 and 8, go in the left-only part. The multiples of 3…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Carroll Diagrams.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A Carroll diagram is another way to sort, but instead of circles it uses a grid of boxes. It sorts items by asking yes or no questions. It is named after Lewis Carroll, who wrote *Alice in Wonderland* and loved puzzles and mathematics. A simple Carroll diagram has two columns and two rows. Suppose we sort the numbers 1 to 12 by two rules: 'Even?' across the top and 'More than 6?' down the side. The top row is 'More than 6' and the bottom row is 'Not more than 6'. The left column is 'Even' and the right column is…

questions.question: A tally shows these counts: 19, 13, 5, 15, 4. What is the total?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.explanation: 9 appears more than any other value, so it is the mode.

selfAssessment: I can read and interpret data shown in a pictogram, using the key to work out real amounts.
I can draw a pictogram from given data, choosing a sensible symbol and key.
I can read and interpret a bar chart (block graph), including its title, axes and scale.
I can draw a bar chart with equal bars, equal gaps and a scale that starts at zero.
I can choose a sensible scale (counting in 1s, 2s, 5s or 10s) for a set of data.
I can sort items and numbers into a Venn diagram, including the overlapping middle.
I can sort items and numbers into a Carroll diagram using yes/no criteria.
I can answer real questions from data: most, least, total, and 'how many more'.
